summary: Steve Yegge argues that dynamic languages are poised for a performance renaissance thanks to advanced JIT compilation techniques like polymorphic inline caching and trace trees. He contends that static type systems have reached their limits and increasingly get in the way of large-scale programming, while runtime optimization can achieve better results than static compilation. The talk traces how the AI winter killed dynamic language research, but the Ajax revolution has reignited interest in optimizing JavaScript and other dynamic languages. Despite these technical advances, Yegge pessimistically concludes that language popularity rankings will remain frozen due to programmer inertia and organizational politics.
key_points: Runtime information enables far better optimization than static compilation - the JIT can inline polymorphic methods, predict types, and undo/redo optimizations based on actual execution patterns
Static type systems have failed to deliver on their promise - they add complexity without making languages more expressive, and generic type errors are often incomprehensible
Language adoption is 80% politics and 20% technology - engineers will literally scream at you for suggesting non-mainstream languages, and switching languages is career-threateningly risky
Tools for dynamic languages can achieve feature parity with static IDEs using data-flow analysis and runtime information - the claim that 'no type tags means no information' is false
Small systems are easier to optimize globally than large systems - Geoworks lost to Microsoft despite hand-optimized assembly because they couldn't see how their 15 million line system worked anymore
money_quotes: So javac, the Java compiler: what does it do? Well, it generates bytecode, does some optimizations presumably, and maybe tells you some errors. And then you ship it off to the JVM. And what happens to that bytecode? First thing that happens is they build a tree out of it... The next thing that happens in the JVM is the JIT undoes all the optimizations! Why? So it can do better ones because it has runtime information.
The languages we have today, sorted by popularity at this instant, are probably going to stay about that popular for the next ten years. Sad, isn't it? Very, very sad. But that's the way it is.

You've never been in a company where there's an engineer with a Computer Science degree and ten years of experience, an architect, who's in your face screaming at you, with spittle flying on you, because you suggested using D. Or Haskell. Or Lisp.
Small systems are not only easier to optimize, they're possible to optimize. And I mean globally optimize.
I realized early in my career that I would actually rather have a runtime error than a compile error. Because the debugger gives you this ability to start poking and prodding.
When you go from Java to JavaScript or Python, you switch into a different mode of programming, where you look a lot more carefully at your code.

All you need is C or C++. All you need is a Turing machine, right? What we're talking about here is fundamentally a very personal, a very political, sort of a, it's almost a fashion statement about who you are, what kind of language you pick.
key_insight: The future of language performance lies in runtime optimization, not static type systems - compilers will eventually get smart enough to make all the tricks programmers learned obsolete.
